title: Custom Error Classes đźŹ—ď¸Ź
description: Build dedicated exception types for your app's specific needs.

slides.title: Why Custom Errors? đź¤·
slides.content:
If you are strictly building a Banking App, throwing a generic `RuntimeError` or `ArgumentError` when a withdrawal fails isn't extremely descriptive.

Imagine if you could explicitly throw a perfectly-named `InsufficientFundsError`?

slides.title: Building the Bomb đź§¨
slides.content:
Because Exceptions are absolutely literally just Objects, you can brilliantly create your own Custom Error simply by creating an empty Class that perfectly inherits from the master `StandardError` class!

```ruby
class InsufficientFundsError < StandardError
end
```
It inherits all the internal crash logic automatically!

slides.title: Custom Catching
slides.content: Run this! We created our own highly specific logic-driven error class, raised it aggressively, and perfectly rescued it cleanly using its exact name!
slides.codeSnippet:
class InsufficientFundsError < StandardError; end

begin
  balance = 100
  withdraw = 500
  
  if withdraw > balance
    raise InsufficientFundsError.new("You broke!")
  end
rescue InsufficientFundsError => error_data
  puts "Transaction Declined: #{error_data.message}"
end

puts "Program finished happily!"

quizData.question: If you brilliantly want to construct your very own Custom Error class called `BankError`, what single massive parent class MUST you mathematically inherit from?
quizData.explanation: Inheriting `class BankError < StandardError` grants your empty class all the violent crashing and rescuing capabilities it needs to actually function as a true throwable error.

slides.title: The Safety Net Shape đź•¸ď¸Ź
slides.content:
Why explicitly inherit from `StandardError` and not the literal absolute master top-level `Exception` class itself?

Because a naked `rescue` block ONLY catches `StandardError` and its children! If you boldly inherited straight from `Exception`, perfectly standard blank `rescue` blocks would completely miss your custom error!

quizData.question: If you write `rescue` incredibly bare, totally without mentioning any specific Exception Class name like `rescue TypeError`, what massive massive default class does it instantly implicitly catch?

options.text: It literally never actually catches anything unless you perfectly name it.

quizData.explanation: A blank standard `rescue` specifically perfectly defaults to catching `StandardError` and absolutely any subclass derived directly from it (including basically all standard ruby errors).
